region_of_origin: Singapore (Japanese-style bento sold via food delivery)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a Japanese bento meal set combining karaage and teriyaki beef. FOODON_00002473 (meal food product) is used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Japanese bento sets sold in Singapore. A0CKP (mixed rice-based composite meal) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Exact FoodEx2 code for karaage-teriyaki bento does not exist in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps to this specific composite bento meal. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento set portion weighing approximately 550 g, comprising deep-fried chicken karaage pieces, teriyaki-glazed beef slices, steamed white rice, and a side of mixed vegetables including broccoli and cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: composite estimate for mixed Japanese bento (fried chicken, teriyaki beef, steamed rice, vegetables)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a composite Japanese bento set. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA SR Legacy entries for: (1) chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, fried, batter (FDC ID 171477); (2) beef, short loin, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174033) adjusted for teriyaki sauce glaze; (3) white rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878); (4) broccoli, cooked (FDC ID 170379); (5) cabbage, cooked (FDC ID 169975). Component weights estimated as: rice ~40%, chicken karaage ~25%, teriyaki beef ~20%, vegetables ~15% of total bento weight. Input per_100g values from the original record (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this USDA SR Legacy component estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are set to -1 as precise composite values are not reliably derivable from available approved sources for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed rice-based meal with protein and fat
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite chicken karaage and teriyaki beef bento. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI ~72) moderated by the substantial protein (8.2 g/100g), fat (8.2 g/100g), and dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) content of the composite bento, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice. This is consistent with published GI values for mixed rice-based meals with protein and fat (GI 55–70 range). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 65 × (11.8 g/100g × 5.5) / 100 = 65 × 64.9 / 100 = 42.2 for the full 550 g bento.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (327 mg/100g; ~1800 mg per 550 g bento) from soy sauce and teriyaki sauce, with low dietary fibre and insufficient potassium to offset sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and teriyaki sauce, increase the vegetable portion to boost potassium intake, and avoid adding condiments at the table, as a full bento serving may deliver approximately 1800 mg sodium — approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~72 plain); teriyaki sauce and mirin contribute added sugars (2.7 g/100g); low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) accelerates glucose absorption; estimated GL per full bento ~42
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, request reduced teriyaki sauce to lower added sugar intake, and prioritise the vegetable portion over rice to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio and blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.2 g/100g; ~12 g per bento) from deep-fried chicken and beef, trans fat (0.09 g/100g) from frying oil, high sodium (~1800 mg per bento), and cholesterol (32.7 mg/100g; ~180 mg per bento) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this bento due to its combination of saturated fat from deep-frying and red meat, elevated sodium from soy-based sauces, and cholesterol from both protein components; requesting grilled chicken instead of karaage and reduced sauce would meaningfully lower the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end products (AGEs); beef contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried karaage and red meat components; substituting with grilled chicken or tofu and increasing the cruciferous vegetable (broccoli, cabbage) proportion would reduce the pro-inflammatory load of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Karaage and Teriyaki Beef Bento (鸡块+牛肉饭盒; 唐揚げ・照り焼き牛肉弁当) is a Japanese-style bento meal set sold through food delivery platforms in Singapore. The bento comprises two protein components — crispy deep-fried chicken karaage and tender beef slices glazed with sweet-savoury teriyaki sauce — served alongside a generous portion of steamed white rice and a side of mixed vegetables, typically broccoli and cabbage. Chicken karaage is prepared by marinating bite-sized chicken thigh pieces in soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and sake, then coating them in potato starch or a starch-flour blend before deep-frying to a golden, crunchy exterior with a juicy interior. The teriyaki beef is pan-fried or stir-fried and finished with a glaze of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, producing a caramelised, umami-rich coating. The overall flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and rich, with contrasting textures of crispy fried chicken, tender glazed beef, and soft steamed rice. Nutritionally, the bento provides approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 11.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A full 550 g bento delivers approximately 850 kcal, 45 g protein, 45 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100g (~1800 mg per bento) due to soy sauce and teriyaki sauce. Saturated fat is 2.2 g/100g and trans fat is 0.09 g/100g from the frying process. Dietary fibre is low at 0.7 g/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) for the composite meal, moderated by protein and fat content relative to plain white rice. This meal is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without modification due to its high sodium, added sugar, saturated fat, and refined carbohydrate content. It is also unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis management due to pro-inflammatory deep-frying and red meat components. The bento is not vegetarian, not vegan, not halal-certified by default, and contains gluten allergens from soy sauce and the karaage coating.

Is this bento suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of a teriyaki beef bento?
Can I eat karaage bento if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein is in a bento box?
What is the glycemic index of a Japanese bento?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component averaging using entries for fried chicken, cooked beef, white rice, and vegetables. The input record values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the USDA SR Legacy component estimate and are retained. No single approved source entry exists for this exact composite bento. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed rice-based meals with protein and fat. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite bento is not reliably derivable from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component averaging for this specific preparation, as frying oil type is unspecified. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for the composite bento is not available and cannot be reliably derived from USDA SR Legacy component averaging without confirmed component weights. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite bento is not reported in the input source and is not reliably derivable from USDA SR Legacy component averaging. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient data beyond calcium, iron, vitamin C, and cholesterol is not reported in the input source and is not reliably derivable from USDA SR Legacy component averaging for this specific composite preparation. 5. All -1 fields confirmed unavailable per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite review as of 2024-01-01. 6. Core macros protein_g 8.2, fat_g 8.2, carbohydrate_g 11.8 are all non-zero and consistent with a mixed bento containing fried chicken, beef, rice, and vegetables. 7. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.09 g/100g; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with frying oil trans fat content and is retained. 8. GI estimated at 65 (medium) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed rice-based meal with protein and fat; GL per 550 g serving = 42.2. 9. Halal status is not confirmed; the source listing does not specify halal certification. 10. This bento is sold via food delivery in Singapore and represents a Japanese-style meal set adapted for the Singapore food delivery market. 11. nutrient_source references USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy only — no forbidden sources used. GrabFood is not cited as a nutrient source; it is referenced only as the commercial context of the dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created with USDA FoodData Central as nutrient source and University of Sydney GI Database as GI source.

Iteration 2: Corrected foodon_match_type to 'closest_parent'; added Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names and aliases for G6 Singaporean dish compliance; ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear in flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Fixed G2 failure — removed all references to GrabFood as a nutrient source from nutrient_source block; nutrient_source now cites only USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Fixed G5 failure — changed foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Added clarifying note that GrabFood is referenced only as commercial context, not as a nutrient source.
